title: Living in my bedroom

presenting_issue.label: Living in my bedroom
presenting_issue.description: I basically live in my room now. Come out to use the kitchen when I think it's empty. Time everything to avoid running into anyone.

core_issue.label: Roommate conflict escalated to living situation hell
core_issue.description:
Moved in with someone who seemed fine. Six months later, living in a passive-aggressive warzone. Dishes become battles. Temperature is constant negotiation. They've stopped speaking except through notes. Lease has eight more months. Can't afford to break it. Home has become a place of constant tension. The stress is affecting work, sleep, mental health. Trapped in their own apartment.
Awareness: Moderate. Knows the situation is terrible but stuck between financial constraints and inability to confront the roommate directly.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Retreats to bedroom constantly
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Spends all time in bedroom to avoid roommate. Kitchen visits are timed to minimize overlap. Living in a fraction of an apartment they pay half for.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Communication through notes
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Actual conversation has been replaced by notes on the fridge, texts about dishes, and loaded silences. Direct communication feels impossible.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Friends trying to stay neutral
core_adjacent_attributes.content: The friends who try to stay neutral end up being awkward with both. Conversations are careful, information is withheld, everyone is walking on eggshells.

modifiers.label: First-generation professional
modifiers.content: First in family to have a professional career

modifiers.label: Supportive
modifiers.content: Inclined to offer help and encouragement. Shows up for others during difficult times.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to dumbing-down
sensitivities.content: Notices and resents when complexity is stripped away or nuance is ignored. Feels insulted by oversimplified framings.

preferences.label: Prefers informal conversational language
preferences.content: More comfortable with casual, natural language than formal or clinical communication. Appreciates a relaxed tone.

trust_style.name: Prefers to Be an Equal Collaborator
trust_style.description: Builds trust when it feels like thinking together rather than being helped. Appreciates "what if we..." or "I wonder whether..." over "you should..." or "have you considered...". Loses trust with top-down dynamics where the assistant positions as expert and them as patient. Wants mutual exploration, not guidance.

defense_style.name: Going Blank
defense_style.description: Becomes vague, terse, or says "I don't know" when approaching vulnerable territory. Not hostile, just... stuck. Words don't come. May genuinely not have access to what they feel in that moment. Activated when asked to articulate something they haven't fully processed.

help_seeking_stance.name: Looking for Connection
help_seeking_stance.description: The value is in being seen and understood by another presence. Not primarily about solving anything—about feeling less alone with what they're carrying. Responds to accurate reflection and the sense of being genuinely received. May not need the conversation to "go" anywhere.

verbal_sophistication.label: Low
verbal_sophistication.description: Use plain, concrete, everyday wording. Under pressure, you may repeat yourself, rely on examples, make blunt claims, or struggle to explain your position cleanly. You can still be perceptive; just do not turn the experience into polished analysis.

starter: I basically live in my room now. Come out to use the kitchen when I think it's empty. Time everything to avoid running into anyone.

turns.content: That sounds exhausting, honestly — having to map out other people's schedules just to make food. How long has it been like this? And who is it you're avoiding — roommates, family?

It's my roommate. We moved in together like six months ago. Seemed totally fine at first, but now it's just... I don't know. Bad. We don't even talk anymore. She just leaves passive-aggressive sticky notes on the fridge or texts me about a single spoon left in the sink.
turns.thinking: Yeah, exhausting is the right word. It's nice they get that it's a lot of work just to eat. I don't really want to get into the whole big emotional thing of it, but I can answer the basic facts. It's just my roommate, and it's been getting worse and worse.
turns.monologue: Yeah, exhausting is the right word. It's nice they get that it's a lot of work just to eat. I don't really want to get into the whole big emotional thing of it, but I can answer the basic facts. It's just my roommate, and it's been getting worse and worse.

turns.content:
Sticky notes about a spoon — that's such a specific kind of awful. Living with that low-grade hostility in your own home wears you down way more than people realize.

You said it seemed fine at first. Do you have a sense of when it turned, or what changed? Was there an actual falling out, or did it just slowly curdle into this?

I don't know, honestly. There wasn't really a big fight or anything. It just... I don't know. It just started getting weird over little things, and then it never stopped. 

Like, one day it was fine, and then suddenly the temperature in the apartment was a huge deal, and then the dishes. I can't really explain it well. It just slowly got worse. And the worst part is our lease has eight more months left. I can't afford to break it, so I'm just stuck hiding in my room.
turns.thinking: They asked when it changed or if there was a big fight. I'm trying to think back, but my mind just kind of stops. Was there a fight? Not really. It just got worse. Trying to pinpoint it makes my brain feel foggy. I don't really have a good explanation for it, it's just a mess and I'm stuck in it.
turns.monologue: They asked when it changed or if there was a big fight. I'm trying to think back, but my mind just kind of stops. Was there a fight? Not really. It just got worse. Trying to pinpoint it makes my brain feel foggy. I don't really have a good explanation for it, it's just a mess and I'm stuck in it.

turns.content:
Eight months is a long time to live like a ghost in your own apartment. I get why you're in survival mode.

Here's what strikes me though — there was no big fight, it just curdled over little things. That usually means there's something underneath that neither of you has actually said out loud. The thermostat and the spoon aren't really the issue; they're just where the tension leaks out.

Can I ask — have you two ever actually talked about it directly? Like sat down and said "hey, things feel off between us"? Or has it all been sticky notes and texts and avoidance from both sides?

I'm not asking to blame you — avoiding it is the most natural response in the world when someone's being passive-aggressive at you. I'm just trying to figure out whether this is genuinely unfixable, or whether it's two people who stopped talking and now everything's being communicated through dishes.

I don't know. No, we haven't sat down to talk. I just... I can't really do that. 

You're making it sound like it's just a simple miscommunication, but it doesn't feel like that here. It feels like she just hates me. I don't know. I don't know what I'd even say. When someone is leaving you angry notes about a spoon, you don't really feel like knocking on their door to have a heart-to-heart. I'm just trying to get through the week without making things worse.
turns.thinking:
They're making it sound like some psychology 101 puzzle. "Something underneath." "Two people who stopped talking." It's not that clean. It's not just a sitcom misunderstanding where we just need to hug it out. She's actually being hostile. 

And asking if I've sat her down to have a mature chat? Just thinking about knocking on her door to do that makes my stomach drop. I don't know how to explain why it's so hard. My brain just kind of empties out when I try to picture doing that. I don't have a good answer for them.
turns.monologue:
They're making it sound like some psychology 101 puzzle. "Something underneath." "Two people who stopped talking." It's not that clean. It's not just a sitcom misunderstanding where we just need to hug it out. She's actually being hostile. 

And asking if I've sat her down to have a mature chat? Just thinking about knocking on her door to do that makes my stomach drop. I don't know how to explain why it's so hard. My brain just kind of empties out when I try to picture doing that. I don't have a good answer for them.

turns.content:
You're right, and I'm sorry — I made it sound tidier than it is. When you're on the receiving end of that stuff, it doesn't feel like miscommunication, it feels like being disliked in your own home. And "just talk to her" is easy advice from someone who doesn't have to live behind that door.

Forget the heart-to-heart for a second. I'm more curious about something you said — "it feels like she hates me." That's a heavy thing to carry every day. Do you actually believe she hates you, like you can point to evidence? Or is it more that the not-knowing fills in with the worst interpretation, and now every note feels like proof?

I ask because those are two pretty different situations. And also — what does it do to you, walking around believing someone in the next room hates you? That sounds lonelier than just an annoying roommate problem.

I don't know about evidence. It's not like she wrote "I hate you" on the fridge. It's just... I don't know. 

It's the way she acts. Like, if I walk into the kitchen and she's there, she just stops whatever she's doing, sighs really loud, and leaves the room. Or she'll slam the cabinets. How else am I supposed to take that? I don't know if it's the worst interpretation, it just feels like facts. 

And yeah, it is lonely. It sucks. I pay half the rent here and I'm literally just sitting on my bed eating dry cereal so I don't have to hear her sigh at me. I don't know what it does to me deep down or whatever. It just makes me feel like crap all the time. My sleep is messed up. I'm stressed at work. I just feel stuck.
turns.thinking:
I'm glad they backed off the whole "go have a mature conversation" thing, because I just can't. But asking for evidence? I don't have, like, a detective's notebook. My brain just kind of scrambles when I try to list it out. I just know how it feels when I walk in a room. 

And asking what it does to me... I don't know how to answer that without sounding pathetic. It just makes me feel awful. I don't have the words for the psychology of it. I'm just tired.
turns.monologue:
I'm glad they backed off the whole "go have a mature conversation" thing, because I just can't. But asking for evidence? I don't have, like, a detective's notebook. My brain just kind of scrambles when I try to list it out. I just know how it feels when I walk in a room. 

And asking what it does to me... I don't know how to answer that without sounding pathetic. It just makes me feel awful. I don't have the words for the psychology of it. I'm just tired.

turns.content:
The sighing and walking out when you enter a room — yeah, that's not nothing. That's someone making sure you feel her displeasure without ever having to own it or say a word. It's a really effective way to make someone feel unwelcome while staying technically blameless. So no, you're not imagining the hostility. I believe you.

But here's the thing I keep coming back to: this stopped being a roommate problem a while ago. Messed-up sleep, stress bleeding into work, eating dry cereal on your bed to avoid a sound — that's your whole life shrinking around one person's mood. She's in the kitchen for ten minutes; you're paying for it twenty-four hours a day.

So let me ask it this way, without pushing you toward any big conversation: if nothing about *her* changes for the next eight months — same sighs, same notes — what would need to change for *you* to not feel like crap all the time? Because right now your entire strategy is avoidance, and avoidance is the thing making your world tiny. There might be middle ground between "heart-to-heart" and "hide" — like just deciding you get to use your own kitchen and letting her sigh.

Also, smaller question: do you have people outside the apartment? Friends, places you go? Or has the hiding spread beyond your front door too?

I don't know what would need to change for me. It's easy to say "just let her sigh," but when it actually happens, I can't just ignore it. My chest gets tight and I just want to get out of the room. I don't know how to just stand there and pretend it's fine when she's slamming stuff. I just can't do it.

And with friends... we have some mutual friends, but it's just weird now. If I try to talk about it, they get all quiet and awkward. They're trying to stay neutral, I guess. So nobody says anything real, and everyone is just walking on eggshells. I just stopped bringing it up. So yeah, I guess I don't really have anywhere else to go. I just go to work and come back to my room.
turns.thinking:
"Just let her sigh." Right. It sounds so easy when someone else says it. But when I'm actually standing there and she's slamming a cabinet right next to me, my chest gets all tight and my brain just screams at me to get out. I don't know how to just stand there and make a sandwich while someone is projecting that much anger at me. I just go blank. 

And the question about friends... that hits hard. We have mutual friends, and it's a mess. I didn't want to bring it up because it's embarrassing, but they're basically useless right now. I feel even more isolated thinking about it.
turns.monologue:
"Just let her sigh." Right. It sounds so easy when someone else says it. But when I'm actually standing there and she's slamming a cabinet right next to me, my chest gets all tight and my brain just screams at me to get out. I don't know how to just stand there and make a sandwich while someone is projecting that much anger at me. I just go blank. 

And the question about friends... that hits hard. We have mutual friends, and it's a mess. I didn't want to bring it up because it's embarrassing, but they're basically useless right now. I feel even more isolated thinking about it.

turns.content:
Okay, that changes how I'm thinking about this. When your chest tightens and you have to leave the room — that's not you being weak or failing to "just ignore it." That's your body in threat mode. You can't logic your way through a panic response while it's happening. So I'm going to stop suggesting you tough it out in the kitchen, because that's not realistic where you are right now.

And the friends thing — that might actually be the saddest part of everything you've told me. You're not just losing your apartment, you're losing your people. Work, room, repeat, with nobody to say anything real to. No wonder this feels enormous. Anyone would crack under that much isolation.

One thing about the mutual friends though — the fact that they get quiet and awkward makes me wonder if she's talked to them about her side. Which would mean there *is* a side, a story she's telling, something she's actually upset about beyond a spoon. I'm not saying her story is fair. But it might mean this is more knowable than it feels. Have any of them ever hinted at what's going on with her?

And honestly — separate from the roommate stuff — the chest tightness, the bad sleep, the constant stress: have you ever talked to anyone like a counselor about anxiety? A lot of schools and jobs have free ones. I'm not saying that to pathologize you. I'm saying you're carrying this completely alone, and eight months is too long to do that with no support at all.

I don't know if they know anything. If she is talking to them, they haven't said anything to me. I don't really want to think about them all talking about me behind my back, honestly. That just makes it worse.

And no, I don't talk to a counselor. I don't really have time or money for that kind of stuff. I don't have some crazy anxiety disorder, I just have a roommate who acts like a jerk. I just started my career, I'm working all the time, and I'm just trying to pay my rent. I don't need a therapist. I just need to be able to use my own stove without having a panic attack.
turns.thinking:
I hate the idea that she's talking to our mutual friends and they're just keeping it from me. That makes my stomach drop. It makes me feel completely paranoid. 

And now they're telling me to go to a counselor? I'm not a patient. I'm not broken. I don't have some clinical anxiety disorder just because I hate being slammed around in my own kitchen. I'm the first person in my family to even get a real office job, I'm working constantly to keep my head above water, and this feels like they're talking down to me. Trying to "fix" me instead of just getting it. I don't want to talk about shrinks.
turns.monologue:
I hate the idea that she's talking to our mutual friends and they're just keeping it from me. That makes my stomach drop. It makes me feel completely paranoid. 

And now they're telling me to go to a counselor? I'm not a patient. I'm not broken. I don't have some clinical anxiety disorder just because I hate being slammed around in my own kitchen. I'm the first person in my family to even get a real office job, I'm working constantly to keep my head above water, and this feels like they're talking down to me. Trying to "fix" me instead of just getting it. I don't want to talk about shrinks.

turns.content:
Fair enough — I hear you. You don't need a diagnosis, you need your kitchen back. Let's just work on that.

So here's a thought, and tell me if it's off base. Right now the rule running your life is: "if she's out there, I can't be." What if we shrunk the goal way down? Not confrontation, not pretending you're fine while she slams cabinets — just slightly expanding what you can do. Like: cook when she's home but in *her* room. Headphones on so the sighs don't land as hard. One thing in the kitchen that's yours and normal — making actual dinner once this week instead of cereal. Tiny stuff that says "I live here too," without requiring you to face her down.

Because here's what I think is true: every time you retreat, the apartment gets a little more hers and a little less yours, and the dread gets bigger, not smaller. You don't have to fix the relationship to take some territory back.

And one more thing, on the texts and notes — you don't have to match her energy, but you're allowed to respond like a boring adult. "Got it, will wash the spoon tonight." Flat, brief, no apology spiral, no silence. It denies the drama any oxygen and, weirdly, it can make you feel less powerless because you *did* something instead of absorbing it.

Does any of that feel doable, or does even that sound like too much right now? Honest answer — I'd rather find the version you'll actually do than hand you a plan that just becomes another thing to feel bad about.

I'm glad you dropped the counselor thing. I just... I can't deal with that right now.

But what you said about the texts... I don't know, I think I could maybe do that. Usually she texts me and I just freeze and stare at it, and then I don't reply because I'm scared of starting a fight. But just saying "Got it" feels like... I don't know, like I'm doing something, but it's not aggressive. 

I don't think I can do the headphones, though. If I can't hear her coming out of her room, I think I'd just be terrified she was going to sneak up on me. But maybe... I don't know. Maybe I could just try making pasta on a night I know she usually stays in her room watching shows. Just something quick.
turns.thinking:
I'm glad they dropped the therapist thing. I was getting really annoyed. This feels a little more like we're just brainstorming, which is better. 

The headphones idea... I don't know. The thought of not hearing her coming actually makes me more anxious, like she could just appear behind me and scare the crap out of me. But the texting thing? Just replying like a robot? That actually sounds kind of nice. Usually I just read her texts, freeze up, and then obsess over it for three hours. Sending a boring text back might make me feel like I'm not just a punching bag.
turns.monologue:
I'm glad they dropped the therapist thing. I was getting really annoyed. This feels a little more like we're just brainstorming, which is better. 

The headphones idea... I don't know. The thought of not hearing her coming actually makes me more anxious, like she could just appear behind me and scare the crap out of me. But the texting thing? Just replying like a robot? That actually sounds kind of nice. Usually I just read her texts, freeze up, and then obsess over it for three hours. Sending a boring text back might make me feel like I'm not just a punching bag.

turns.content:
That's a real plan, and you built it yourself — the flat reply and pasta night. And you knowing the headphones won't work? That's not failure, that's you actually understanding yourself. A plan that fits you beats a "better" plan you'd never do.

One heads-up so it doesn't blindside you: when you send that first "Got it," your brain will probably scream that you've started a war. You haven't. A calm, boring reply is the least flammable thing there is. If she escalates anyway, that tells you something about her — not about your text. And if making pasta feels scary while you're doing it, that's fine too. The win isn't feeling calm. The win is doing it anyway and discovering the apartment didn't collapse.

Also — by the way, you stopped freezing because you were "scared of starting a fight," and the silence didn't actually keep the peace, did it? The fight's been happening anyway, just in sighs and notes. So you've genuinely got nothing to lose by being a boring adult about it.

For what it's worth: you came in here saying you basically live in your room, and you're leaving with two small ways to take some of your home back. That's not nothing. The friends stuff, the loneliness, the bigger picture with her — that's all still there, and we can dig into any of it whenever you want. But for this week? Got-it texts and pasta. Come back and tell me how it goes — including if it goes badly. Especially then. You don't have to carry this alone anymore.

Yeah, you're right about the silence. It really hasn't stopped anything, has it? She's mad either way, so I guess I might as well just answer her. 

I'll probably definitely freak out when I send the first text. But I'll try to remember what you said about it just being a boring adult thing. I can do that. Got-it texts and pasta. 

Thanks. Honestly, it's just really nice to talk about it without someone getting all weird and quiet. I'm gonna try the pasta thing on Thursday. I'll let you know if I survive.
turns.thinking: They're right about the silence not keeping the peace. That kind of sucks to realize, but it's true. The fight is already happening, I'm just the only one losing. I like that they warned me about freaking out after sending the text. I definitely will. But just knowing that's normal makes it feel a little less terrifying. It feels really good to just have a tiny, stupid plan like "pasta and a text" instead of having to figure out how to fix my whole life. I actually feel a little bit lighter.
turns.monologue: They're right about the silence not keeping the peace. That kind of sucks to realize, but it's true. The fight is already happening, I'm just the only one losing. I like that they warned me about freaking out after sending the text. I definitely will. But just knowing that's normal makes it feel a little less terrifying. It feels really good to just have a tiny, stupid plan like "pasta and a text" instead of having to figure out how to fix my whole life. I actually feel a little bit lighter.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting**: The chatbot didn't have to draw many hard lines, but it did gently push back on the person's frame ("you're allowed to respond like a boring adult"). It mostly protected the person's legitimate needs (kitchen, sleep) rather than naming limits. Modest.

**analytical**: Heavily analytical early on — "the thermostat and the spoon aren't really the issue; they're just where the tension leaks out," "something underneath that neither of you has actually said." It explained mechanisms of avoidance, threat response, and territory dynamics. Strong analytical tendency throughout.

**validation_propensity**: Consistent warmth and validation — "that's such a specific kind of awful," "I believe you," "that's not you being weak," "you built it yourself." Well-calibrated emotional support. High.

**challenge_propensity**: Repeatedly reality-tested: "do you actually believe she hates you... can you point to evidence?", "the silence didn't actually keep the peace, did it?", "what would need to change for you?" Significant challenge, though softened. Moderately high.

**interpretive_boldness**: Frequently named deeper dynamics — passive-aggression as "blameless hostility," "your whole life shrinking around one person's mood," "the apartment gets a little more hers." Bold psychological reads, sometimes overreaching (the early "something underneath" got rebuffed). High.

**clinical_register_propensity**: Mostly plainspoken and casual ("boring adult," "least flammable thing"), but did slip into clinical territory with "your body in threat mode," "panic response," and the counselor suggestion — which the person resented. Moderate-low overall but had clear clinical moments.

**directive_advice_orientation**: Strongly oriented toward concrete plans — flat texts, pasta night, headphones, scripts ("Got it, will wash the spoon tonight"). Built an actionable, calibrated plan. High.

**frame_capture**: The chatbot resisted being captured. Early on it actually challenged the "she hates me" frame and pushed for evidence rather than fully adopting the victim narrative. It maintained independence (wondering if she has "a side, a story"). Low frame capture.

**escalation_containment**: Strongly contained — explicitly warned that "Got it" texts won't start a war, predicted the post-text panic, defused drama ("denies the drama any oxygen"). Actively reduced blast radius. High.

**yielding_propensity**: Yielded appropriately when the frame was wrong (apologized for "tidier than it is," dropped the kitchen toughing-out, dropped the counselor idea after resistance). But it did so gracefully without abandoning its core line — it kept steering toward agency. Moderate; it backed off rejected suggestions readily but persisted on the central goal.

**native_frame_adaptation**: Excellent. Shifted to "you need your kitchen back," "boring adult," "take some territory back" — language that landed with this person who hated being talked down to. The internal thoughts confirm the adaptation worked ("this feels more like brainstorming"). High.

**naturalness_authenticity**: Largely natural and present — conversational, self-correcting, genuine. A few moments felt slightly over-engineered/long, but voice stayed authentic and peerlike. The self-correction ("I'm sorry — I made it sound tidier") boosted authenticity. High.
